title: Part-Based Attention Models
concept: Part-based attention models locate and zoom in on key local areas of an object to extract fine-grained features.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why is background or color sometimes a dangerous cue in fine-grained classification?

options.text: Because the model might rely on background cues rather than actual object features.
Because color logits cannot be normalized.
Because background pixels are always processed as zeros.

exercises.explain: Correct! Background patterns are easy to overfit to, distracting the model from key fine-grained details.

cards.title: Finding the clues
cards.body: If you want to identify a specific bird, you look at its beak, its eyes, and its tail feathers. You don't just stare at the whole bird at once. Neural networks can be trained to look at these specific parts too!

cards.title: Part-Based Attention
cards.body: Part-based attention models use internal neural mechanisms (like attention maps or regional proposals) to identify key localized components of an object. The network extracts features from these specific areas and merges them to make a prediction.

cards.title: A detective's magnifying glass
cards.body: Imagine a detective examining a room. Instead of just taking a wide photo, they use a magnifying glass to look at a fingerprint on the doorknob, a smudge on the glass, and a dropped key. The magnifying glass represents part-based attention.

cards.title: Bird species localization
cards.body: When classifying birds, an attention-guided network outputs local heatmaps for the [head, wing, feet]. It crops these regions in feature space and passes them through a secondary classifier to find the exact species.

cards.title: No manual cropping needed
cards.body: You might think engineers have to manually draw boxes around heads and wings for thousands of images to train this. Modern attention networks learn where these parts are automatically, guided only by the final class label (weakly supervised learning).

cards.title: Attention Map Focus
cards.body: How attention maps isolate regions of interest:

left.text:
Sees the entire bird and sky
Features are diluted
Hard to find subtle markers

right.text:
Highlights beak and crown
Filters out sky and branch
Extracts sharp local features

exercises.prompt: What is the primary function of part-based attention models in computer vision?
exercises.explain: Attention models focus the network's processing power on key areas (like a bird's beak) containing critical diagnostic details.

options.text: To identify and extract features from specific, informative regions of an object
To double the spatial resolution of the input image
To delete all background pixels and replace them with black
To convert the model into a single-label architecture

exercises.prompt: True or false: Modern part-based attention models require humans to manually label the coordinates of every object part during training.
exercises.explain: False. Modern models use self-attention or weakly supervised learning to discover key parts without manual bounding boxes.
Heatmaps show which parts of the image contribute most to the network's final decision.
exercises.sentence: Attention heatmaps highlight regions of high ___ for the classification task.

exercises.prompt: Which of these outputs represents a part-based attention network's localization?
exercises.explain: Highlighting a specific diagnostic part like a taillight indicates part-based attention at work.

options.text: A heatmap highlighting the unique taillight shape of a sedan
A bounding box around the entire car
A prediction probability score of 95% for the class 'Sedan'

exercises.prompt: You are building a system to classify retail products (like specific shampoo bottles). The bottles are visually identical except for the tiny text listing the flavor. How can part-based attention help?

options.text: It can automatically locate and crop the text label region, feeding a zoomed-in, high-resolution crop to the classifier.
It can change the colors of the bottle to make them look different.
It can force the model to ignore the text entirely.
It can reduce the number of channels from 3 to 1.

exercises.explain: Exactly! By isolating the text region and extracting features specifically from it, the model bypasses the identical bottle shapes and colors, solving the fine-grained task. Next, let's look at how we merge different feature extractors mathematically!
